revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:外官]]
'''Gaikan''' (外官) refers to offices and officials outside the capital under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}. It stands in contrast to {{ill|Kyōkan|ja|京官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōkan|1=Central officials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10883145}} (or Naikan).
== Overview == Gaikan encompassed the offices and positions such as {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|qid=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu (government)}}, provincial {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}}, {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}, {{ill|Corps|en|Corps|qid=Q188497|lt=Gundan|1=Corps}}, and {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku (education under the Ritsuryō system)|1=Kokugaku}}, as well as other local offices. The {{ill|Settsu Province Administration|ja|摂津職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsu Province Administration|1=Settsu-shiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65269215}} was an exception, treated as equivalent to Kyōkan.
In China, regional governance traditionally fell to lords under the feudal system. With the transition to the Ritsuryō system, Gaikan replaced the role of feudal lords, carrying out local governance while remaining subject to inspections and mandates from the emperor or his emissaries. These officials regularly reported to the imperial court, presenting tributes (九貢) at ceremonies such as the New Year’s court assembly.
Japan adopted the Chinese system but introduced unique distinctions. Gaikan, such as the officials of Dazaifu and provincial governors, were appointed by the emperor, distinguishing them from the Gunji, who were often recruited from local elites or former {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|en|Kuni no miyatsuko|qid=Q2483673|lt=Kuni no miyatsuko|1=Kuni no miyatsuko}}. For instance, under the {{ill|Kushikiryō|ja|公式令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushikiryō|1=Kushikiryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11392034}}, imperial decrees to Gaikan bore the {{ill|Inner Seal|ja|内印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Seal|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 御璽|1=Privy Seal of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2915131}} (the emperor's personal seal), while those to Kyōkan used the {{ill|Outer Seal|ja|外印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Seal|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太政官印|1=Outer Seal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17225808}} (the Dajōkan's seal). This distinction emphasized the direct authority of the emperor over Gaikan.
Furthermore, hierarchical protocols reinforced the special status of provincial governors. For example, when meeting a governor on the road, Gunji and subordinates were required to dismount and show deference (下馬礼). On New Year’s Day, they performed ceremonial obeisance (拝賀) to the governor at the {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11421342}} as the emperor’s representative.
== Compensation and Issues == Gaikan officials received compensation through {{ill|Shokubunden|ja|職分田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokubunden|1=Shokubunden|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11611232}} (land for official duties). Dazaifu officials and those in {{ill|Iki Province|en|Iki Province|qid=Q1052938|lt=Iki Province|1=Iki Province}} and {{ill|Tsushima Province|en|Tsushima Province|qid=Q1054861|lt=Tsushima Province|1=Tsushima Province}} were also granted seasonal stipends ({{ill|Koroku|ja|季禄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koroku|1=Kiroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11448057}}) and permitted to cultivate fallow land. They were further entitled to {{ill|Kōkae|ja|公廨稲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkae|1=Kōkae|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11392029}} (tax rice for official use).
Due to the lucrative nature of these positions, many sought Gaikan roles solely for financial gain, leading to systemic issues such as the creation of {{ill|Ingaikan|ja|員外官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ingaikan|1=Yuanwaikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q55521279}} (extra officials), {{ill|Gongwan|ja|権官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gongwan|1=Gonkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11542306}} (acting officials), and the practice of {{ill|Yōnin|ja|遥任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōnin|1=yōnin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11642494}} (remote appointments where officials did not reside in their assigned regions). During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, Gaikan positions were often sold to supplement the state treasury through mechanisms like {{ill|Nengan|ja|年官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nengan|1=Nengan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11483830}} or {{ill|成功|en|成功|qid=Q11074480|lt=Successorship (appointment)|1=成功}}.
To address these challenges, reforms strengthened the authority of provincial governors ({{ill|Juryō-kokushi|ja|受領国司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Juryō-kokushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 受領|1=zuryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410703}}). These included transitioning from {{ill|Zu Yong Diao|en|Zu Yong Diao|qid=Q839365|lt=Sōyōchō|1=Zu Yong Diao}} to more flexible taxation systems like {{ill|Kanmon|ja|官物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanmon|1=Kanmon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452115}} and {{ill|Zatsumotsu|ja|雑物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zatsumotsu|1=Zatsumotsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11659407}}, as well as replacing the outdated military organization of the Gundan with the {{ill|Kokuga military system|ja|国衙軍制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga military system|1=Kokuga military system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11421343}}.
== References == * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Gaikan," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6426904}} Volume 5, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985. ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoyo, "Gaikan," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Volume 2, Heibonsha, 1993. ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Tokinoya Shigeru, "Gaikan," in {{ill|Heian Jidaishi Jiten|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Jidaishi Jiten|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11482399}}, Kadokawa Shoten, 1994. ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7. * Torao Tatsuya, "Gaikan," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524488|1=Nihon Rekishi Daijiten}} Volume 1, Shōgakukan, 2000. ISBN 978-4-095-23001-6.
{{translated page|ja|外官|version=91064981|comment=Imported full ja history}}
